cases.title: Minerva Mills Ltd. v. Union of India
legal_principles: Harmony between Fundamental Rights and DPSP
cases.summary: Struck down clauses 4 and 5 of the 42nd Constitutional Amendment which had sought to give absolute primacy to Directive Principles over Fundamental Rights and to immunise constitutional amendments from judicial review. The Court reaffirmed the Basic Structure Doctrine from Kesavananda Bharati and held that the balance between Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles is itself part of the basic structure.
key_holdings: Balance between Fundamental Rights (Part III) and Directive Principles (Part IV) is part of the basic structure
Parliament cannot abrogate Fundamental Rights to implement Directive Principles
Judicial review of constitutional amendments is preserved
Clauses 4 and 5 of 42nd Amendment declared unconstitutional
key_quotes: The Constitution is founded on a nice balance between Part III and Part IV. To destroy that balance is to destroy an essential element of the Constitution.
Courts are the guardians of the Constitution.
cases.why_important: Reaffirmed and strengthened Kesavananda Bharati's basic structure doctrine. Struck down attempts to give Parliament unlimited amending power and immunise amendments from judicial review.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Important for understanding limits on government power that may impact financial regulations and tax laws.

cases.title: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India
cases.summary: The government impounded the petitioner's passport without giving reasons. The Court expanded the scope of Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) to include the right to go abroad. It held that 'procedure established by law' must be right, just, and fair â€” not arbitrary, fanciful, or oppressive. The case linked Articles 14, 19, and 21 as a 'golden triangle' of rights.
key_holdings: Right to travel abroad is part of 'personal liberty' under Article 21
'Procedure established by law' must satisfy standards of natural justice
Articles 14, 19, and 21 are not mutually exclusive â€” they form an integrated charter of rights
Any law affecting personal liberty must be just, fair, and reasonable
key_quotes: The law must be right, just and fair and not arbitrary, fanciful or oppressive.
Basic rights cluster around man in his individual capacity and in his social life.
cases.why_important: Landmark expansion of Article 21 beyond mere physical liberty. Established that fundamental rights are interlinked and that state action must satisfy substantive due process.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Establishes procedural fairness standards relevant to tax enforcement and financial penalty proceedings.

cases.title: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India
cases.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that imposition of President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review. The court held secularism and federalism are essential features of the basic structure of the Constitution. It restrained the arbitrary misuse of Article 356 by requiring the President's satisfaction to be based on relevant material.
key_holdings: Imposition of President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review
Secularism is a basic feature of the Constitution
Federalism is part of the basic structure
Floor test must be held before recommending President's Rule
Court can restore dismissed government if imposition was unconstitutional
key_quotes: Secularism is the basic feature of the Constitution.
The floor of the House is the place to test majority.
cases.why_important: Drastically curtailed arbitrary use of Article 356 by the Union government against state governments. Firmly established secularism and federalism as basic structure elements.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to centre-state financial relations and GST council disputes under cooperative federalism.

cases.title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
legal_principles: Supreme Court's power to issue guidelines in absence of legislation
relevant_acts: Prevention of Sexual Harassment at Workplace Act 2013 (later enacted)
cases.summary: Filed following gang rape of social activist Bhanwari Devi. The Supreme Court filled a legislative vacuum by issuing the 'Vishaka Guidelines' â€” binding directions on employers to prevent and address sexual harassment at the workplace. The guidelines remained in force until POSH Act 2013 was enacted.
key_holdings: Sexual harassment at workplace violates Articles 14, 15, 19(1)(g), and 21
Court can issue binding guidelines to fill legislative vacuum
Employers have a duty to prevent sexual harassment
Internal Complaints Committees to be established
International obligations (CEDAW) can inform domestic law
key_quotes: Gender equality includes protection from sexual harassment.
Where legislation is absent, the Supreme Court can issue binding guidelines.
cases.why_important: Landmark judgment creating sexual harassment framework at workplace; directly led to enactment of POSH Act 2013. Established that courts can fill legislative gaps on human rights issues.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to HR compliance obligations for companies â€” POSH compliance is a legal mandate for all organisations.

cases.title: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain
legal_principles: Free and fair elections as basic structure
cases.summary: After Allahabad High Court found PM Indira Gandhi guilty of corrupt practices in elections, the 39th Amendment retroactively validated her election and placed disputes regarding PM's election beyond judicial review. Supreme Court struck down the amendment as violating free and fair elections â€” part of the basic structure.
key_holdings: Free and fair elections are part of the basic structure of the Constitution
Parliament cannot validate its own elections retrospectively
Rule of law and separation of powers are basic structure elements
Clause 4 of 39th Amendment struck down as unconstitutional
key_quotes: Democracy postulates that free and fair elections must be held.
Rule of law is the basic feature of our Constitution.
cases.why_important: Applied and extended basic structure doctrine to election law. Led directly to declaration of Emergency in 1975.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Establishes that legislative overreach cannot validate improper financial or administrative decisions retrospectively.

cases.title: Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation
legal_principles: Right to livelihood as part of Right to Life
cases.summary: Pavement dwellers challenged eviction from Bombay's footpaths. The Court held that the right to livelihood is included in the right to life under Article 21 because no person can live without the means of living (livelihood). The state cannot deprive a person of livelihood without following a just and fair procedure.
key_holdings: Right to livelihood is part of the right to life under Article 21
Eviction without opportunity to be heard violates Article 21
Pavement dwellers cannot be evicted without alternative arrangements
The sweep of the right to life is wide and far-reaching
key_quotes: The right to life includes the right to livelihood.
An easement like right has been acquired over footpaths by pavement dwellers.
cases.why_important: Established right to livelihood as a constitutional right under Article 21. Foundation for numerous public interest litigations on housing and employment rights.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Right to livelihood impacts labour law and employment-related financial decisions.

cases.title: Union Carbide Corporation v. Union of India
relevant_acts: Bhopal Gas Leak Disaster (Processing of Claims) Act 1985
cases.summary: Settlement of â‚¹715 crore (USD 470 million) for victims of the 1984 Bhopal gas tragedy that killed thousands. The Court approved the settlement between UCC and Government of India acting as parens patriae for victims. It also made doctrine of absolute liability applicable â€” industrial enterprises engaged in hazardous activities are absolutely liable for harm caused.
key_holdings: Doctrine of absolute liability applies to enterprises engaged in hazardous activities
Government can act as parens patriae to represent disaster victims
Courts have jurisdiction to approve class-action type settlements
Amount of compensation must be 'just, equitable and reasonable'
key_quotes: Enterprises which are engaged in hazardous activity owe an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community.
There are no exceptions to the principle of absolute liability.
cases.why_important: Established absolute liability doctrine (stronger than strict liability from Rylands v Fletcher). Set precedent for corporate liability in industrial disasters. Led to Environment Protection Act and related legislation.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Absolute liability doctrine directly impacts corporate insurance requirements and financial risk provisioning.

cases.title: M.C. Mehta v. Union of India (Oleum Gas Leak Case)
cases.summary: Following the oleum gas leak from Shriram Foods and Fertiliser Industries in Delhi, M.C. Mehta filed a PIL. The Supreme Court laid down the doctrine of Absolute Liability: an enterprise engaged in hazardous activity is absolutely liable to pay compensation if any harm results â€” with no exceptions as under the English rule of strict liability from Rylands v. Fletcher.
key_holdings: Absolute liability doctrine: no exceptions apply to enterprises causing harm through hazardous activity
Enterprise must be absolutely liable, regardless of whether harm occurred despite all precautions
Amount of compensation should be commensurate with the magnitude and capacity of enterprise
Public interest litigation is a powerful tool to enforce environmental rights
key_quotes: We are of the view that an enterprise which is engaged in a hazardous or inherently dangerous industry... owes an absolute and non-delegable duty to the community.
We do not think that the rule of strict liability as evolved in Rylands v Fletcher is adequate for modern conditions.
cases.why_important: Created India's unique doctrine of absolute liability for hazardous industries â€” stronger than English strict liability. Foundation of modern Indian environmental law.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Environmental liability principles affect corporate financial reporting, insurance, and compliance costs.

cases.title: Unni Krishnan J.P. v. State of Andhra Pradesh
legal_principles: Right to education as fundamental right
cases.summary: The Supreme Court held that right to education up to age 14 is a fundamental right under Article 21, read with Articles 41 and 45 of the Directive Principles. It also laid down a scheme (fee structure) for private engineering and medical colleges. Later, the 86th Constitutional Amendment (2002) gave explicit recognition to right to education, leading to the Right to Education Act 2009.
key_holdings: Right to education up to age 14 is a fundamental right under Article 21
State must provide free and compulsory education up to 14 years
Private educational institutions can charge fees but not capitation fees (profiteering prohibited)
Education is not a business; it is a charitable activity
key_quotes: Right to education flows directly from right to life.
Capitation fee amounts to commercialisation of education which is opposed to our constitutional scheme.
cases.why_important: Recognised right to education as fundamental right â€” paved way for 86th Amendment and Right to Education Act 2009. Regulated capitation fees in private colleges.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to education-related tax exemptions (Section 10 of Income Tax Act) and compliance requirements for educational institutions.

cases.title: Indian Young Lawyers Association v. State of Kerala (Sabarimala Case)
legal_principles: Exclusion based on physiological characteristics is unconstitutional
relevant_acts: Kerala Hindu Places of Public Worship (Authorisation of Entry) Act 1965
cases.summary: The Supreme Court (4:1) struck down the practice of excluding women aged 10-50 from entering the Sabarimala temple. The majority held this practice violated Articles 14, 15, and 17 (untouchability), and that 'constitutional morality' must prevail over popular religious morality.
key_holdings: Exclusion of women from temple based on biological/physiological characteristics is unconstitutional
Constitutional morality must prevail over popular morality
Right to worship is guaranteed to all, regardless of gender
Exclusionary religious practices that violate fundamental rights are not protected under Article 26
Dissent (Justice Indu Malhotra): Courts should not interfere in matters of religion
key_quotes: Devotion cannot be subjected to notions of purity and pollution based on physiological phenomena.
Constitutional morality is not natural to a society; it has to be cultivated.
cases.why_important: Landmark judgment on intersection of religious freedom and gender equality. Generated significant social debate. Review petitions referred to 9-judge bench.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Demonstrates how constitutional rights interact with institutional practices â€” relevant to compliance frameworks.

cases.title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
legal_principles: Right to privacy includes sexual orientation
State cannot criminalise consensual adult behaviour
cases.summary: Unanimously decriminalised consensual same-sex relations among adults by reading down Section 377 IPC. Overruled Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013) which had reinstated Section 377. The Court held that criminalising consensual adult same-sex conduct violates Articles 14, 15, 19, and 21.
key_holdings: Section 377 IPC unconstitutional to the extent it criminalises consensual adult same-sex conduct
Sexual orientation is a protected ground under Article 15 (discrimination)
Right to privacy includes right to one's sexual orientation
Criminalising consensual adult relationships violates dignity and autonomy under Article 21
Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013) overruled
key_quotes: An individual's sexual orientation is a natural phenomenon.
History owes an apology to members of the LGBT community.
cases.why_important: Landmark judgment decriminalising homosexuality in India. Affirmed dignity and autonomy as core constitutional values.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Anti-discrimination compliance obligations for employers now extend to sexual orientation under evolving law.

cases.title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Right to Privacy Case)
legal_principles: Right to privacy is a fundamental right
cases.summary: 9-judge bench unanimously declared that the right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21. Overruled M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) which had held privacy is not a fundamental right. Paved the way for Aadhaar challenge and India's Personal Data Protection framework.
key_holdings: Right to privacy is a fundamental right protected under Article 21
Privacy includes autonomy over personal choices, informational privacy, and bodily integrity
State can restrict privacy only through law, legitimate aim, and proportionality
Data protection is a facet of right to privacy
Private entities can also violate privacy rights
key_quotes: Privacy is the ultimate expression of the sanctity of the individual.
The right to be let alone is the beginning of all freedom.
cases.why_important: Landmark unanimous ruling declaring privacy a fundamental right. Foundation for India's data protection law and restraints on Aadhaar-based surveillance.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: foundational for financial data privacy â€” bank account linking, PAN-Aadhaar, and RBI data localisation requirements.

cases.title: Bandhua Mukti Morcha v. Union of India

parties.petitioner: Bandhua Mukti Morcha (Bonded Labour Liberation Front)

legal_principles: Right to live with human dignity
cases.summary: Bandhua Mukti Morcha wrote letters to the Supreme Court about bonded labourers in Haryana quarries. The Court treated the letters as writ petitions (epistolary jurisdiction) and ordered release of bonded labourers. Held that right to live with dignity is a part of right to life under Article 21 and bonded labour violates this right.
key_holdings: Bonded labour is unconstitutional under Article 23
Right to live with human dignity is part of Article 21
Court's epistolary jurisdiction allows letters to be treated as PILs
State must rehabilitate released bonded labourers
Minimum wage violation amounts to forced labour under Article 23
key_quotes: Article 21 includes the right to live with basic human dignity.
Letters addressed to the court can be treated as writ petitions.
cases.why_important: Expanded the scope of PIL by allowing letters to be treated as petitions. Linked minimum wage compliance to Article 23's prohibition on forced labour.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Minimum wage compliance is a legal obligation with tax and financial implications for employers.

cases.title: Mohd. Ahmed Khan v. Shah Bano Begum
legal_principles: Section 125 CrPC applies to all citizens regardless of religion
Personal law cannot override secular law
relevant_acts: Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act 1986
cases.summary: Shah Bano, a divorced Muslim woman, claimed maintenance under Section 125 CrPC. Husband claimed Muslim personal law (mehr was sufficient). Supreme Court held that Section 125 CrPC applies to all Indian citizens regardless of religion and upheld Shah Bano's right to maintenance. Court also commented on the desirability of a Uniform Civil Code.
key_holdings: Section 125 CrPC applies to all citizens regardless of religion
Divorced Muslim women are entitled to maintenance under CrPC
Payment of mehr does not absolve husband of maintenance obligation
Court expressed desirability of Uniform Civil Code under Article 44
key_quotes: Section 125 of the Code of Criminal Procedure is a secular provision which applies to all communities.
It is a matter of regret that Article 44 has remained a dead letter.
cases.why_important: Generated enormous controversy and led to enactment of Muslim Women (Protection of Rights on Divorce) Act 1986 by Congress government, partly overturning the ruling. Sparked debate on uniform civil code.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Financial obligations in personal law (maintenance, alimony) have income tax and financial planning implications.

cases.title: State of Madras v. Champakam Dorairajan
cases.summary: Supreme Court struck down Madras Communal G.O. fixing seats in state educational institutions based on religion and caste. It was the first case where Supreme Court struck down a law for violating fundamental rights. Led directly to First Constitutional Amendment adding Article 15(4) allowing reservations.
key_holdings: Communal G.O. reserving seats by caste/religion violates Article 15(1) and 29(2)
Directive Principles (Article 46) cannot override Fundamental Rights
First Constitution Amendment then added Article 15(4) to enable reservations
cases.why_important: First case striking down law for violating FRs; directly caused First Constitutional Amendment adding positive discrimination clause.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Reservation law impacts HR hiring costs, compliance obligations, and government contract eligibility.

cases.title: Indra Sawhney v. Union of India (Mandal Commission Case)
cases.summary: Upheld 27% OBC reservation recommended by Mandal Commission but imposed 50% cap on total reservations and excluded 'creamy layer' (economically advanced OBCs) from benefits. Struck down 10% reservation for economically backward among forward castes.
key_holdings: Total reservation cannot exceed 50% (except in extraordinary circumstances)
Creamy layer among OBCs must be excluded from reservation benefits
Reservation in promotions not permitted (Article 16(4A) added later)
Economically backward among forward castes reservation struck down
27% OBC reservation upheld
cases.why_important: Definitive judgment on OBC reservation; set 50% ceiling and creamy layer doctrine still governing reservation law today.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: OBC reservation impacts government procurement policies and public sector employment compliance.

cases.title: Commissioner of Income Tax v. Calcutta Knitwears
legal_principles: Survey statements are not conclusive admissions
Right to retract statements made during survey
cases.summary: The Supreme Court held that statements recorded during a survey under Section 133A of Income Tax Act cannot be used as conclusive admissions. Assessee has right to retract such statements and department must independently verify income through evidence.
key_holdings: Statement recorded under Section 133A during survey is not a sworn statement
Assessee can retract statement made during survey
Tax authorities cannot solely rely on survey statements â€” must corroborate with other evidence
Voluntary disclosure has evidentiary value but is not conclusive
cases.why_important: Protects taxpayers from coerced admissions during income tax surveys. Clarifies evidentiary weight of survey statements.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to tax assessment, audit compliance, and rights of taxpayers during IT surveys.

cases.title: Mohit Minerals Pvt. Ltd. v. Union of India
legal_principles: GST Council recommendations are not binding law
relevant_acts: Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act 2017
cases.summary: Struck down the government notification levying IGST on ocean freight for imported goods on reverse charge basis. The Court held this amounted to double taxation (freight included in CIF value already taxed through Customs). Also held that GST Council recommendations are recommendatory and not binding â€” Parliament and state legislatures retain legislative power.
key_holdings: IGST on ocean freight under reverse charge mechanism on CIF imports is unconstitutional (double taxation)
GST Council recommendations are recommendatory and not binding on Parliament or state legislatures
Constitutional scheme preserves sovereignty of Parliament and state legislatures
Notification imposing IGST on ocean freight struck down
key_quotes: The GST Council is a recommendatory body and its recommendations do not have the force of law.
Double taxation of the same economic activity is impermissible.
cases.why_important: Landmark GST case clarifying power of GST Council and striking down double taxation on ocean freight. Highly relevant to importers.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to GST compliance, import tax planning, and understanding limits of GST Council authority.

cases.title: Canon India Pvt. Ltd. v. Commissioner of Customs
legal_principles: Proper officer jurisdiction in customs matters
Section 28 demand must be by proper officer who first assessed
cases.summary: Supreme Court quashed customs demand notices issued by DRI (Directorate of Revenue Intelligence) officers against Canon India for alleged evasion on camera imports. Held that DRI officers who had not assessed the goods at the time of import cannot issue demand notices under Section 28 of the Customs Act â€” only the 'proper officer' who assessed the goods has such jurisdiction.
key_holdings: DRI officer who did not assess goods cannot issue demand notice under Section 28 of Customs Act
Jurisdiction of 'proper officer' under Section 28 is that officer who first assessed the goods
Demand by a non-proper officer is without jurisdiction and void
Customs Act amended prospectively to address this (Finance Act 2022)
cases.why_important: Led to Finance Act 2022 amendment empowering DRI officers retrospectively. Significant for customs duty evasion cases.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to customs duty disputes, import compliance, and DRI notices for importers.

cases.title: Commissioner of Income Tax v. Kelvinator of India Ltd.
legal_principles: Reopening of assessment requires tangible new material
Change of opinion not grounds for reassessment
cases.summary: Supreme Court held that after the 2001 amendment to Section 147, the Assessing Officer cannot reopen a completed assessment merely on the basis of a 'change of opinion'. There must be 'tangible material' to form the belief that income has escaped assessment. This remains the leading case on Section 147 reassessment.
key_holdings: Post-2001, Section 147 requires 'tangible material' for reopening â€” mere change of opinion is not sufficient
Where assessment was made under Section 143(3), there is a presumption against reopening without new material
AO cannot act as a review authority on his own completed assessment
Distinction between 'formation of belief' and 'change of opinion'
key_quotes: After the 1989 amendment, the power to reopen is much wider, but one important safeguard is that there must be 'tangible material' to support the belief.
Reason to believe is not the same as reason to suspect.
cases.why_important: The most-cited Supreme Court judgment on income tax reassessment. Prevents arbitrary reopening of completed assessments by tax department.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Very directly relevant â€” governs when Income Tax department can reopen completed assessments. Critical for tax planning.

cases.title: Vodafone International Holdings BV v. Union of India
cases.summary: Vodafone purchased Hutchison's Indian telecom assets through offshore acquisition of Cayman Islands holding company. Indian government sought capital gains tax on the Hutchison-Vodafone deal (~â‚¹11,000 crore). Supreme Court ruled in Vodafone's favour â€” offshore transaction involving transfer of a foreign company's shares not taxable in India. Government then amended IT Act retrospectively (Finance Act 2012) to tax such deals. The retrospective amendment was later reversed by Finance Act 2021.
key_holdings: Offshore transfer of shares of foreign holding company not taxable in India under pre-2012 law
Revenue cannot look through a transaction â€” should look at the transaction
Tax department cannot recharacterise a legitimate commercial transaction
Every taxpayer has the right to structure affairs to minimize tax
key_quotes: The income-tax department cannot tax a non-resident on offshore transactions.
A citizen has the right to structure his affairs in a manner which would legitimately reduce the incidence of tax.
cases.why_important: Historic tax case involving â‚¹11,000 crore demand. Led to Finance Act 2012 retrospective amendment (overturning the judgment), and then Finance Act 2021 reversing the retrospective amendment. Basis for India's international tax reforms.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Extremely relevant to cross-border transactions, FDI structures, and capital gains tax planning.

cases.title: Commissioner of Income Tax v. Reliance Industries Ltd.
cases.summary: Clarified the treatment of depreciation under Section 32 of the Income Tax Act for 'block of assets'. Held that the written down value (WDV) of the entire block must be calculated and depreciation applies to the block as a whole, not individual assets. Where WDV becomes nil or negative, no depreciation is allowable.
key_holdings: Depreciation under Section 32 applies to 'block of assets' as a whole
Written Down Value (WDV) calculated for the entire block
When WDV becomes zero or negative, no depreciation is admissible
Short-term capital gain/loss on sale of asset calculated by reference to the block
Slump sale provisions and depreciation interact differently from individual asset sales
cases.why_important: Definitive ruling on depreciation calculations for businesses. Directly impacts income tax computation for all businesses with fixed assets.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to tax calculations for businesses â€” depreciation claims affect taxable income.

cases.title: Union of India v. VKC Footsteps India Pvt. Ltd.
legal_principles: Rule 89(5) GST Rules restricts refund of ITC on input services
No refund for accumulated ITC on input services under inverted duty structure
relevant_acts: Central Goods and Services Tax Act 2017
cases.summary: Supreme Court held that Rule 89(5) of CGST Rules, which restricts refund of ITC accumulated on input services under inverted duty structure, is constitutionally valid. Taxpayers in inverted duty structure (where input tax rate is higher than output tax rate) cannot claim refund of ITC attributable to input services, only input goods.
key_holdings: Rule 89(5) limiting ITC refund to input goods (excluding input services) is constitutionally valid
Section 54(3) of CGST Act permits Parliament to restrict refund by rules
Inverted duty structure refund available only for ITC on inputs (goods), not input services
Taxpayers with inverted duty structure must plan their ITC utilisation accordingly
cases.why_important: Major GST ruling affecting manufacturers and traders with inverted duty structures â€” particularly impacting footwear, textiles, and pharmaceuticals sectors.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to GST refund planning, working capital management, and ITC utilisation strategies.

cases.title: Sahara India Real Estate Corporation Ltd. v. SEBI

parties.petitioner: Sahara India Real Estate Corporation Ltd.
parties.respondent: Securities and Exchange Board of India

legal_principles: SEBI has jurisdiction over OFCDs (Optionally Fully Convertible Debentures)
Public offer regulations apply even through private placement route if issued to 50+ subscribers
cases.summary: Sahara raised over â‚¹24,000 crore from millions of small investors through Optionally Fully Convertible Debentures (OFCDs), claiming these were private placements exempt from SEBI regulation. Supreme Court held that SEBI had jurisdiction and ordered Sahara to refund â‚¹24,000 crore (+ 15% interest) to investors. This led to arrest of Subrata Roy.
key_holdings: SEBI has jurisdiction over OFCDs as they are 'securities' under the Securities Contracts Act
Issuance to 50+ persons constitutes a public offer requiring SEBI compliance
Refund of â‚¹24,000+ crore with 15% interest ordered
Private placement cannot circumvent public offer regulations
Investor protection is paramount in securities law
key_quotes: What cannot be done directly, cannot be done indirectly.
The primary role of the Securities Regulator is to protect the investors.
cases.why_important: Landmark investor protection case. Led to reforms in private placement regulations and strengthened SEBI's enforcement powers.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to SEBI compliance, capital market regulations, and investor protection frameworks.

cases.title: Committee of Creditors of Essar Steel India Ltd. v. Satish Kumar Gupta

parties.petitioner: Committee of Creditors of Essar Steel India Ltd.

legal_principles: Commercial wisdom of Committee of Creditors is paramount
Courts cannot interfere with CoC business decisions
Distinction between financial and operational creditors in waterfall
cases.summary: Landmark IBC case. Supreme Court held that the NCLAT erred in directing equal treatment of operational and financial creditors in the Essar Steel resolution plan. Affirmed that the Committee of Creditors' (CoC) commercial wisdom on distribution under resolution plan is supreme and cannot be interfered with by courts or NCLT/NCLAT, except on specified limited grounds. Also set 330-day outer limit for CIRP completion.
key_holdings: Commercial wisdom of CoC is paramount and cannot be substituted by NCLT/NCLAT
Operational creditors do not have equal rights as financial creditors in distribution
Resolution plan must ensure operational creditors receive at least liquidation value
CIRP must be completed within 330 days including litigation time
Section 29A disqualification of promoters/connected persons upheld
key_quotes: The commercial wisdom of the CoC has been given paramount status with no judicial interference.
Dissenting financial creditors must be given liquidation value as minimum.
cases.why_important: Definitive ruling on IBC resolution process, CoC's powers, and creditor hierarchy. Essential for understanding India's insolvency framework.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Directly relevant to banking NPA resolution, debt restructuring, and creditor rights under IBC.

cases.title: Swiss Ribbons Pvt. Ltd. v. Union of India
legal_principles: Distinction between financial and operational creditors is reasonable
cases.summary: Constitutional validity of IBC upheld. The Court held that the differentiation between financial creditors (banks/NBFCs) and operational creditors (suppliers/employees) is constitutionally valid and based on intelligible differentia. IBC's object of revival and resolution over liquidation upheld.
key_holdings: IBC is constitutionally valid
Distinction between financial and operational creditors is constitutionally valid under Article 14
Resolution preferred over liquidation serves legitimate state aim
Section 29A disqualification of promoters is valid
Time-bound CIRP process is constitutionally valid
cases.why_important: Upheld the constitutional validity of IBC â€” foundational judgment for India's insolvency regime.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Foundation for understanding India's insolvency law, NPA resolution, and creditor hierarchy.

cases.title: SEBI v. Kishore R. Ajmera

parties.petitioner: Securities and Exchange Board of India

legal_principles: Preponderance of evidence in SEBI proceedings
Circumstantial evidence sufficient for insider trading
cases.summary: SEBI held trader guilty of insider trading based on circumstantial evidence â€” trading in shares of a company immediately before announcement of merger, having connection to persons in possession of unpublished price-sensitive information (UPSI). Supreme Court upheld SEBI's order and affirmed that circumstantial evidence and preponderance of probability standard applies in SEBI proceedings (not 'beyond reasonable doubt').
key_holdings: SEBI proceedings are quasi-judicial â€” preponderance of probability standard applies (not criminal 'beyond reasonable doubt')
Circumstantial evidence is sufficient to prove insider trading
Connections to UPSI holders combined with suspicious trading patterns establishes guilt
SEBI can impose civil penalties based on circumstantial chain of evidence
cases.why_important: Clarified the evidentiary standard in SEBI proceedings. Confirmed SEBI can use circumstantial evidence to prove insider trading â€” significant for securities law enforcement.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Critical for understanding SEBI compliance and insider trading laws applicable to listed companies.

cases.title: Reserve Bank of India v. Jayantilal N. Mistry
legal_principles: RBI's fiduciary duty to banks does not override RTI obligations
cases.summary: RBI resisted RTI disclosure of bank inspection reports citing Section 8(1)(e) of RTI Act (fiduciary relationship). Supreme Court held that RBI does not stand in a fiduciary relationship with banks â€” it is a regulator, not an advisor. Therefore, inspection reports and related information must be disclosed under RTI Act, subject to specific exemptions.
key_holdings: RBI is not in a fiduciary relationship with banks â€” it is a regulator
Section 8(1)(e) exemption (fiduciary relationship) does not protect RBI from RTI disclosures
Inspector General/RBI reports can be disclosed unless specifically exempted
Public interest in banking transparency outweighs confidentiality claims
RTI Act applies fully to RBI's regulatory activities
cases.why_important: Significant for banking transparency and RTI rights of citizens to access banking regulatory information.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to banking compliance, RBI inspection obligations, and financial transparency requirements.

cases.title: A. Ayyasamy v. A. Paramasivam
legal_principles: Fraud allegations do not automatically make dispute non-arbitrable
Serious allegations of fraud â€” court jurisdiction reserved
Commercial disputes with fraud preferred to be arbitrated
cases.summary: Held that mere allegations of fraud do not make a dispute non-arbitrable and courts cannot refuse to refer parties to arbitration on that ground alone. Only complex, serious fraud allegations that require detailed investigation warrant court proceedings. Simple fraud allegations in commercial disputes should be referred to arbitration.
key_holdings: Mere allegation of fraud does not oust arbitral jurisdiction
Courts should refer commercial disputes with fraud allegations to arbitration unless fraud is serious and complex
Serious fraud â€” where public interest is involved or where fraud pervades the entire contract â€” may not be arbitrable
Strong pro-arbitration policy of Indian courts
cases.why_important: Landmark judgment on arbitrability of fraud disputes. Promotes alternative dispute resolution in commercial matters.
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: Relevant to financial contract disputes, banking fraud cases, and arbitration clauses in commercial agreements.

cases.title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
relevant_articles: Article 13 - Abolition of laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights
cases.summary: In this landmark case, the Supreme Court held that the principle that emerges from several earlier judgments is that, although Article 368 describes the procedure for amendment and the subject matter of amendments, the Constitution is nonetheless subjected to the limitations imposed on its amendment by the Constitution itself. The Constitution being a body of fundamental law, the courts' duty is to expound that fundamental law and defend it against infringement. The article 368 as it stands before us must be read as subject to these implied limitations. The Parliament cannot use the Article 368 of the Constitution to alter the basic structure of the Constitution. The basic framework cannot be destroyed. The Constitution is supreme law and no organ of the State can act in violation of it.
key_holdings: Parliament's amendment power under Article 368 is not unlimited
The Constitution has a basic structure that cannot be amended
Basic structure includes: sovereignty, federalism, democracy, secularism, and fundamental rights
Any amendment attempting to alter the basic structure is void
Courts have power to test constitutionality of amendments
key_quotes: The Constitution is the supreme law of the land and all State organs must function within its framework.
Although the Constitution is a body of fundamental law, it is nevertheless subject to limitations on its amendment imposed by itself.

The Constitution in its Preamble states that the people have resolved to give themselves a Constitution. Such a Constitution would be of a sovereign democratic republic. These ideas are part and parcel of the basic structure of the Constitution.

dissent.main_argument: The amending power is plenary and unrestricted; courts cannot impose implied limitations on Article 368
dissent.outcome: Minority view was rejected but influenced future jurisprudence

impact_and_significance: Limited the amending power of Parliament
Protected fundamental rights from being amended away
Foundation for numerous later constitutional judgments
Referenced in legal education across India
subsequent_cases: Indira Nehru Gandhi v. Raj Narain (1975) - 42nd Amendment
Minerva Mills v. Union of India (1980) - Reaffirmed basic structure
S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994) - Applied basic structure
National Legal Services Authority v. Union of India (2014) - Basic structure and rights
amendments_related: 44th Amendment (1978) - Removed property rights from Part III
cases.why_important: This case is fundamental to Indian constitutional law. It established that even constitutional amendments must respect certain basic principles. Without this doctrine, the government could theoretically amend the Constitution to eliminate democracy, fundamental rights, or federalism. This case protects the integrity of the Indian Constitution against majoritarian overreach.
cases.bar_exam_relevance: Highly relevant for constitutional law in law school and bar exams. Candidates must know this landmark judgment.
cases.accuracy_note: Information gathered from Indian Kanoon and verified against multiple legal sources
cases.relevance_to_legal_finance_system: While primarily about constitutional law, this judgment demonstrates how courts protect fundamental rights including property rights. Relevant to understanding guardrails on government financial/tax regulations.
